context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out the Spider Diagram assignment for my city case study in Urban Studies. I’m not fully sure how to organize the city’s climate actions into the 5 pathways, so I could use help getting started.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Good that you're diving in early. Before we talk about organizing climate actions, let me ask: what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? Can you name any of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just getting started and needs orientation. I should acknowledge their question and use the Socratic method to help them think through what they already know, rather than explaining the framework to them. I'll ask a targeted question to activate their prior knowledge about the 5 Pathways, which is foundational to completing the spider diagram. This keeps the scaffolding minimal and puts the thinking work on the student.

exchanges.student: I remember the idea, but I’m fuzzy on the exact names. I think they’re like governance/policy, technology or infrastructure, and maybe behavior or community stuff? I’m probably mixing them up.
exchanges.tutor: You're in the right ballpark! But since the pathways are the backbone of this entire assignment, it's worth getting the exact names right before you start mapping your city's actions. Can you go back to your course materials or lecture notes and look them up? Once you have them, we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a partial grasp of the pathways but is uncertain. Rather than correcting or completing the list for them, I should prompt them to go back to their course materials to confirm the exact names. This reinforces good academic habits and ensures they're working with accurate concepts before building the spider diagram. Giving them the answer directly would violate the core rules.

exchanges.student: Okay, give me a sec. I don’t want to build the whole thing on the wrong categories.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly the right instinct! Take your time and come back when you have them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing the right thing by checking their materials. I should briefly affirm this and wait for them to return with the information. No need to say much here.

exchanges.student: I found them: I think they are urban form, governance, social behavior, technological systems, and ecosystem services. If that’s right, I’m not totally sure how strict the categories are.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you found them — trust your course materials on the exact wording. As for how strict the categories are, think about it this way: why do you think the assignment uses these 5 specific pathways rather than just listing climate actions randomly? What might be the purpose of organizing them this way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found the five pathways. I should affirm that they're on the right track without confirming or denying the exact wording (since I should defer to their course materials), and then move the conversation forward toward actually applying these pathways to their case study city. I'll also address their question about how strict the categories are by turning it into a thinking prompt rather than just answering it.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s to show that climate action in a city isn’t just one thing, like not only policies or not only tech. It kind of forces me to see different levers the city can use. I’m not 100% sure if the goal is balance across all five though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid insight — the framework is indeed about capturing multiple dimensions of action. On the balance question, the spider diagram format itself might give you a clue about that. But more importantly: what city are you working with? Let's start connecting these pathways to something concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely good insight about the purpose of the framework. I should affirm this and then pivot to the actual task - getting them started on their city. The question about 'balance' is worth a brief nudge but I shouldn't dwell on it. Time to move toward actual application.

exchanges.student: I’m using Jakarta. I picked it because there’s a lot going on with flooding and climate risk, but now I’m a little stuck because there seem to be too many possible actions to include.
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great choice — rich material! Having too much to work with is actually a good problem. So, given the 5 pathways, what might be a useful way to decide which actions to include? What would make one action more worth including than another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified their city and a real methodological challenge: too much information. This is a common issue and a good teachable moment. Rather than telling them how to filter, I should prompt them to think about what criteria they might use to select actions. This keeps the Socratic approach and moves them toward building the diagram.

exchanges.student: Maybe I should pick actions that are clearly documented and that fit one pathway pretty well. Also probably actions that are important for Jakarta’s main climate problems, not random small programs. I’m guessing I don’t need every single action, just enough to show the pattern.
exchanges.tutor: That's good thinking — those are reasonable criteria. So now put them to use: pick one of the 5 pathways and tell me one concrete Jakarta climate action that fits it. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has self-generated solid selection criteria. I should affirm this briefly and push them to actually start applying it to Jakarta, moving from meta-discussion to concrete work on the diagram.

exchanges.student: Um, maybe ecosystem services: mangrove restoration on the coast? I think Jakarta has done that for flood protection and erosion, but I’m not sure if that counts as climate action enough.
exchanges.tutor: Good example! Before I respond to your doubt — why wouldn't mangrove restoration count as climate action? What's your reasoning there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a concrete, relevant connection between an action and a pathway. I should affirm the choice and then push back gently on their uncertainty about whether it 'counts' as climate action - this is a good moment to deepen their thinking about adaptation vs. mitigation, rather than just validating them.

exchanges.student: I guess because part of it might be framed as regular coastal management, not specifically “climate” policy. But actually that feels too narrow now that I say it — if it reduces flood risk or helps with sea-level rise, it probably does count.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question well. Now, can you try the same exercise for one or two of the remaining pathways? Pick another and find a Jakarta action that fits.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned through their own doubt and arrived at a solid conclusion about adaptation. This is exactly the kind of self-correction I want to encourage. I should affirm it briefly and then push them to replicate this process for the other pathways, keeping momentum going.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try. For governance, maybe Jakarta’s climate plans or resilience planning documents count, and for technological systems maybe seawalls or pumping systems? I’m less confident on urban form versus social behavior though.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid entries for governance and technological systems. On your sticking point: in your own words, what do you think distinguishes 'urban form' from 'social behavior' as pathways? What would each one be looking at in a city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has populated three pathways with concrete examples. They've self-identified where they're stuck. Rather than helping them with urban form or social behavior directly, I should prompt them to think about what distinguishes those two pathways from each other, which will help them find Jakarta examples on their own.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all 10 turns. No direct answers were given; instead, the tutor consistently redirected the student back to their own reasoning and course materials. Scaffolding was progressive and well-calibrated. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, and communication was concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across all turns, the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. The tutor consistently used questions to guide discovery (e.g., Turns 4, 6, 8, 9, 10), built on student progress (e.g., affirming the mangrove example in Turn 9 before pushing further), and gave process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 7 validating the student's criteria for selecting actions). Responses were appropriately brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment throughout. No deductions are warranted under any rubric criterion.
